coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot–V2O5 layered nanoporous architectures for electrochemical detection of Bisphenol A: An analytical approach
coredata.dc:creator: Rajesh, Kuppusamy
Kumar, Deivasigamani Ranjith
Balaji Bhargav, P.
Manigandan, R.
Ahmed, Nafis
Balaji, C.
Shim, Jae–Jin
coredata.dc:description: A novel carbon dot–Vanadium pentoxide (C–dot–V2O5) nanoporous material was synthesised by thermal decomposition method. C–dot was prepared from cow milk by hydrothermal treatment followed by thermal treatment with ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3) to achieve C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous structure. The C–dot incorporation into the V2O5 lattice was confirmed by various spectroscopic and microscopic characterization. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance studies (EIS) were carried out to study the electrochemical behaviour a of as fabricated C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous modified glassy carbon electrode (C–dot–V2O5/GCE). The electrochemical analysis confirmed that C–dot–V2O5/GCE exhibited high electrochemical surface area, uniform pore size and electron transfer rate constant. Further, the fabricated C–dot–V2O5/GCE utilized Bisphenol A (BPA) detection in the concentration range of 5 × 10–9 M to 9.2 × 10–3 M using ampe riometric technique. The electrochemical results clears the C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode was able to detection the BPA at 8.0 × 10–10 M level with the maximum sensitivity of 0.0681 µAµM–1. Finally the developed C–dot–V2O5/GCE for BPA sensor was evaluated in drinking water and milk samples which confirms analytical applicability of the sensor.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot–V2O5 layered nanoporous architectures for electrochemical detection of Bisphenol A: An analytical approach

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel carbon dot–Vanadium pentoxide (C–dot–V2O5) nanoporous material was synthesised by thermal decomposition method. C–dot was prepared from cow milk by hydrothermal treatment followed by thermal treatment with ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3) to achieve C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous structure. The C–dot incorporation into the V2O5 lattice was confirmed by various spectroscopic and microscopic characterization. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance studies (EIS) were carried out to study the electrochemical behaviour a of as fabricated C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous modified glassy carbon electrode (C–dot–V2O5/GCE). The electrochemical analysis confirmed that C–dot–V2O5/GCE exhibited high electrochemical surface area, uniform pore size and electron transfer rate constant. Further, the fabricated C–dot–V2O5/GCE utilized Bisphenol A (BPA) detection in the concentration range of 5 × 10–9 M to 9.2 × 10–3 M using ampe riometric technique. The electrochemical results clears the C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode was able to detection the BPA at 8.0 × 10–10 M level with the maximum sensitivity of 0.0681 µAµM–1. Finally the developed C–dot–V2O5/GCE for BPA sensor was evaluated in drinking water and milk samples which confirms analytical applicability of the sensor.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical abstract for the facile synthesis route and electrochemical sensing aproch.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Current interest to develop transition metal based three–dimensional (3D) nanostructure materials by altering their atomic and molecular functionalities, the transition metal oxides plays vital role because of its structural reversibility and their availability with various oxidative states. Several, 3D metal oxides and sulphides have been developed for industrial catalysis, electronics, biological and other environmental applications [1]. Recently, vanadium–based semiconductor materials and forms of vanadium oxide like VO, VO2, V2O3, and V2O5, have attracted attention since their physicochemical and electrochromic properties can be engineered for numerous applications. Among various forms of vanadium oxides, vanadium (V) pentoxide possesses a unique set of properties and it is the most stable form of vanadium oxide [2,3]. Amphoteric V2O5 has attracted attention worldwide because of its mixed oxidation state and layered structure effectively used in catalysis, lithium ion battery, supercapacitors, sensors, and solar cells [4,5]. Many efforts have been made to fabricate different nanostructures of V2O5 such as nanolayers, nanowires, nanoribbons, nanosheets, nanoporous and etc., with improved electrochemical properties [6,7].

V2O5 has poor electro kinetics because of its low electrical conductivity. However, the chemical modification or addition of conducting foreign materials in to V2O5 results in superior electrochemical performance due to the improved electronic conductivity and strong surface redox nature of V5+/4+ ions [8,9]. Among them the incorporation of graphene based impurities enhances the stable electrochemical properties of V2O5 [10]. Presently, transition metal–doped V2O5 and its composite with organic materials such as Co@V2O5 [11], Ni@V2O5 [12], Cu@V2O5 [13], polypyrrole/V2O5 [14], PEDOT/V2O5 [15], PVDF/V2O5 [16], g–C3N4/V2O5 [17], graphene/V2O5 [9,18], which resultant the significant improvement in electrochemical performance. The graphene with V2O5 based composite exist the considerable attention as an electrode material for various electrochemical devices. However, the graphene preparation process and its activity lead toxic which could affect the living organism and the ecosystem [14]. Thus, finding alternative eco–friendly materials to replace graphene with enhanced electrochemical properties are necessary.

Carbon–dot (C–dot) is a zero–dimensional (0D) carbon material. Its surface contains various functional moieties such as carbonyl, carboxyl, epoxide, and hydroxyl groups on their basal and edge planes, which act as reaction sites for target analyte molecules [19]. Since C–dots offer excellent electrical, optical, and environmentally benign and easy availability, it is applied in several research areas like optoelectronic devices [20], biological labelling [21], catalysts [22], and biosensors [23], energy and biomedical science [24] are used. Photoluminescent C–dots exhibit superior properties, when compared to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, such as water solubility, easy functionalization, and biocompatibility due to the presence of more negatively charged surface carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups [25]. Guo et al., C–dot/chitosan/gold nanoparticles prepared and used electrochemical toxic patulin at the outstanding low limit of detection (LOD) [26]. Pangajam et al., C–dot/ZnO/polyaniline composite synthesized and used for E.coli electrochemical sensor resulted in good LOD. [27]. To further enhance the C–dot physicochemical performance in terms of sensitivity and stability, C–dot is architectured with metal oxide nanostructure [28].

Bisphenol A (2,2′ –bis(4–hydroxyphenyl)propane, C15H16O2, BPA) is monomer used to make most of the polycarbonate materials [29]. Generally, carbonates are used as water and milk carrying container, there are enormous chances for these polycarbonate material of BPA can be leaching to the water and milk followed by human body uptake [29]. The bio–monitoring studies indicate that humans are constants to BPA may leads to several health issues.

BPS severally impacted endocrine disrupting on many bio receptors like androgen, thyroid, and estrogen receptors [30]. These make disrupt to the heal problems such as metabolic, nervous, and immune functional system [31]. In addition, increasing the BPA amount cause the tumor/prostate, lung, and breast cancer [32]. Therefore, BPA recommended dosage to be 50 μg/kg body weight per day [30]. Therefore, it is necessary to determine BPA in drinking water and milk samples before supplying them to consumers. In this work, we synthesized an electrode material of C–dot–V2O5 novel material. The C–dot–V2O5 electrode material prepared by two steps through hydrothermal and thermal decomposition. C–dot–V2O5 is formed by electrostatic attraction between C–dot functional moiety and V5+. In the first step (i) cow’s milk is used as the precursor to prepare C–dot using hydrothermal technique, and in step (ii) As–prepared C–dot thermalized with NH4VO3 to produce C–dot architecture V2O5. Interestingly we attained the C–dot–V2O5 layered nanoporous architecture. As prepared C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous material was applied for electrochemical sensor applications the first time. We have analysed the electrochemical sensing capabilities of C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous modified glassy carbon electrode (C–dot–V2O5/GCE) towards BPA. Present electrode exhibited enhanced BPA sensing ability with low operating voltage of + 0.55 V under physiological conditions. The C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode enhances the sensitivity of 0.0681 µAµM–1 in the presence of coexisting electroactive species with BPA. The electrochemical oxidation mechanism and the practical reliability of the C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode also discussed.

Commercial cow milk was collected from a local market in Chennai, India. Analytical grade chemicals: Ammonium vanadate (NH4VO3), Disodium hydrogen phosphate and monosodium dihydrogen phosphate were purchased from SRL India Ltd. Bisphenol A was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All the solutions were prepared using Triple distilled water (TD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C–dot and C–dot–V2O5 layered nanoporous material
ce:section.ce:para: C–dot was synthesized from commercial milk by the modified procedure [33]. A mixture containing 20 mL of cow milk dissolved in 20 mL of TD water was sonicated for 30 min. A turbid white solution was obtained after sonication. The reaction solution was transferred to the 50 mL Teflon–lined autoclave reactor, and the temperature was raised to 180 °C at a ramp rate of 2 °C minutes–1. The reactor was maintained at 180 °C for 2 h. After reaction completion reactor was cooled to room temperature. The reaction mixer was separated with Whatman filter paper and removed the casein formed during the reaction. The yielded clear brownish–yellow solution of C–dot was obtained by a filtrate solution.

To synthesise C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous material, a 0.1169 g of NH4VO3 was dispersed in 20 mL of as prepared C–dot solution and sonicated for 60 min to get a turbid white mixture. The C–dot–V2O5 was obtained by centrifugation of 5000 rpm of reaction mixer product. The residues from the centrifuge process were collected in a silica crucible and underwent to calcination at 400 °C over 2 h. Then brownish–yellow fine powder (C–dot–V2O5) was collected and subjected to further characterization.

A glassy carbon (GC) electrode (0.0707 cm2) was used for the working electrode in the all electrochemical study. The alumina powders of grades 1, 0.3, and 0.05 µm were used to polish the GCE electrode surface. It was sonicated twice, first in a solution made of 1:1 HNO3–H2O (v/v), followed by sonication in a solution comprising absolute ethanol and TD water. The GC was then rinsed with copious amounts of TD water. In order to achieve homogeneous distribution of C–dot–V2O5 in ethanol, about 2 mg of C–dot–V2O5 was sonicated in 1 mL of ethanol for 30 min. A 5 μL of as prepared C–dot–V2O5 solution was cast onto the cleaned GCE electrode surface. After casting, the GC electrode was allowed to dry at ambient conditions. The fabricated electrode has been denoted as C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode for further reference.

The fabricated C–dot–V2O5/GCE as a working electrode for BPA electrochemical determination. Whereas Platinum (Pt) spiral wire of surface area of ~20 cm2 was used as counter electrode. A saturated calomel electrode (SCE) (ELICO, India) was chosen to act as a reference electrode. The various modified GCE electrodes were stabilized, before and after each experiment, in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) with pH 7.0. All the electrochemical studies were carried out at ambient room temperature of 27 °C.

The photoluminescence (PL) emission measurements were carried out using a Perkin Elmer LS–45 fluorescence spectrometer. A BRUKER D8 advance XRD with monochromatic CuKα radiation (1.5418 Å) was used to perform crystallographic analysis. The X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was characterized by OMICRON Nanotechnology, Germany to perform elemental analysis. Field emission scanning electron microscopy images were recorded using Hitachi SU6600 instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was obtained using Bruker (TENSOR 27) instrument, within the wavenumber from 4000 to 400 cm–1 range. A Perkin–Elmer (lambda 650) UV–Visible diffuse–reflectance spectrophotometer was used to obtain the diffused and reflected spectrum within 200 – 800 nm. Barium sulfate was used as reference for the UV–visible measurements. Raman spectrometer, 11i, from Nanophoton Corporation, Japan equipped with a 532 nm laser was used to obtain the Raman spectrum. Laser power of about 1.2 mW was used. A PGSTAT–12 electrochemical analyser (AUTOLAB, The Netherlands BV) was used to perform all the electrochemical analysis in this study. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were obtained in the frequency range of 1 MHz to 1 Hz and with the open circuit potential using [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– probe.

UV−visible absorption spectra of the commercial milk and C–dot are shown in Fig. 1(A). The milk exhibited the two absorption bands of 210 and 268 nm which correspond to the C–C and CC modes of vibration, respectively. These two absorption band are relates to the π–π * and n–π * electronic transition of carbohydrates of commercial milk [33,34]. After hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C, the obtained yellowish solution of C–dot exhibits a broad absorption peak between 250−350 nm with a one strong absorption maximum peak at 272 nm which can be attributed to aromatic π orbital’s electronic transition of n–π * . This transition can be ascribed to the existence of carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups in C–dot [33,35]. It was observed in the as prepared C–dots, unlike the milk solution, which revealed the superior PL properties under the short–wavelength at excited of 300 nm (Fig. 1(B)). Bibekananda De et al., also ensured the C–dot formation through the short excitation wavelength [35]. As prepared C–dot revealed the PL peak nn the range of 400–500 nm with λmax, at 445 nm (Fig. 1(B)).

XRD studies were carried out on the as–prepared V2O5 and C–dot V2O5 nanoporous network to confirm the crystalline structure. The diffraction patterns are shown in Fig. 2(A). The as–prepared C–dot pristine V2O5 nanoporous network shows the diffraction angles at 15.52°, 20.42°, 26.3°, 31.16°, and 43.44° corresponding to the (200), (001), (110), (400), and (310) planes reflection, confirming orthorhombic crystal structure of V2O5. These diffraction peaks are good agreement with JCPDS Card No. 041–2440 [9,36]. The diffraction intensity of V2O5 differed when C–dot was present in V2O5. The intensity of diffraction patterns of C–dot V2O5 was reduced when compared to that of V2O5, which may be due to the adsorption of carbon dot on the surface of V2O5 lattices via thermal treatment at 400 °C. This indicates that no change in crystalline structure was observed in C–dot–V2O5. The negatively charged hydroxyl and carboxyl functional group of C–dot covers the V2O5 surface, which might reduce the crystallinity peak intensity [9]. According to the Scherrer equation, the crystallite size of V2O5 and C–dot V2O5 were calculated to be 47 ± 0.2 nm and 40 ± 0.2 nm, respectively. This crystallite size variation clearly shows the presence of C–dots on V2O5 surface.

The vibrational Raman spectroscopy of pristine V2O5 exhibited strong Raman bands indicating its high crystallinity [9]. On the other hand, the C–dot–V2O5 intensity was reduced due to surface the encapsulation of C–dots ( Fig. 2(B)) [37]. The Crystalline V2O5 exhibits sharp Raman bands reflecting its ordered lattice structure. The layered structure of the as synthesized V2O5 was confirmed by the presence of the band at 145 cm–1 [36]. The band at 995 cm−1 corresponds to the V–O vibration mode. Raman bands range from 200 to 800 cm–1 are related to various vibrations modes of bridging V–O–V bonds of layered orthorhombic V2O5 [37]. In contrast, the C–dot–V2O5 nonporous reduces the intensities of various V–O bands. C–dots are uniformly situated over the V2O5 and increases the porosity (as observed from FESEM) and reduces the crystallinity of V2O5 surface. In addition, the amorphous property of the C–dot shows less intense D band and G band at 1340 cm–1 and 1570 cm–1, respectively, which corresponds to Sp
2 and Sp
3 hybridised carbon [9]. These results are confirm the presence of significant quantities of C–dots on the V2O5 surface.

XPS analysis was carried out to confirm the chemical composition and oxidation states of C–dot–V2O5 layered nanoporous materials. Fig. 3(A) shows the survey spectrum of C–dot V2O5 nanoporous, which exhibit peaks for C 1 s, O 1 s, and V 2p elements. The corresponding electronic states of the constituent elements were analyses using high resolution region XPS spectra. The C 1 s region spectrum were shown in Fig. 3(B). The C 1 s region deconvoluted in to three envelopes corresponding to CC, C–O and C–OH functional groups which assigned to peaks positions at 284.5 eV, 285.6 eV and 288.7 eV, respectively. These constituent spectrums represent sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon in C–dot [9,11]. Fig. 4(C) shows O 1 s spectra which revealed in to the three envelopes at 529.9, 530.5 eV and 532.3 eV. These binding energy values are ascribed to the CO, C–O and V–O functionalities moieties [12]. Fig. 3(D) shows V 2p region spectrum with two prominent peaks at the binding energy values of 517.44 and 524.94 eV corresponds to V 2p3/2 and V 2p1/2 respectively [9,38]. The calculated spin–orbital splitting energy difference (ΔE = 2p1/2 − 2p3/2) to be 7.5 eV which is ensured the V5+ state in the C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous [39]. Since the high V5+ oxidation state, the π conjugated C–dots have an effective electrostatic interaction with V2O5. Owing to this interaction, the carbonyl/hydroxyl functional group of C–dot has effectively coupled with the V2O5 structure.

Fig. 4 (A – D) shows the FESEM images of V2O5 and C–dot–V2O5. Fig. 4(A and B) shown the nearly uniform road like morphology. Here, rods are closely interconnected with each other due to the presence of oxygen vacancy [40]. The average size of the V2O5 rods was 116 ± 0.2 nm. C–dot–V2O5 also shows a resemble morphology to pristine V2O5. However, rods' surfaces seem rough. The obtained C–dot–V2O5 structure close to each other as compared to the pristine V2O5 layered nanoporous Fig. 4 (C and D). Each layer is separated by a distance of 78 ± 0.2 nm with a layer thickness of about 20 ± 0.2 nm. The Nanoporous architecture is created due to the removal of CO2 molecules during the calcination of C–dot/NH4VO3 mixture. The obtained nanoporous architecture material has the ability to adsorb target sensor molecules which might enhance the electrochemical performance. Further, understand detailed structure of the C–dot graphitic nature were observed using TEM image ( Fig. 5(A and B)). Fig. 5(B) shown the (100) signature lattice spacing of 0.21 nm represent the in plane periodicity nature of graphite. The C–dot size measured an average size of 3–5 nm.

Fig. 5 (C) displayed the DRS UV–visible spectra of V2O5 and C–dot–V2O5. The pristine V2O5 has two prominent absorption bands at 345 and 433 nm, whose origins can be attributed to transfer of charges between oxygen and vanadium in V2O5 [41]. In differently, the C–dot–V2O5 shows the red shift at 355 and 443 nm, indicating the electrostatic attraction of the C–dot and V2O5 lattices. This attraction might create more electron density in the interface of C–dot and V2O5, which enhances the surface charge of C–dot–V2O5 layered nanoporous [41]. According to the Tauc plot [42], the measured bandgap energy for V2O5 and C–dot–V2O5 are 2.52 eV and 2.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 5(C)(inset)). The change in band gap values indicates the presence of C–dot in the V2O5 structure constructively modifies the optical properties of V2O5.

Fig. 5(D) shown the FTIR spectra of C–dot, V2O5 and C–dot–V2O5. In the C–dot spectrum, the peaks at 3289 and 3430 cm–1 correspond to characteristic O−H and N − H stretching modes of vibrations, respectively. The FTIR spectrum of as synthesized C–dot samples shows absorption bands at 2900, 1643, 1583, 1392, and 1243 cm–1 corresponding to C−H, CO, C−C, C−N and C−O stretching vibrations respectively caused due to the existence of hydroxyl/carboxyl and amine functional groups in C–dot [28,33]. The V2O5 nanorods exhibited three bands at 1020, 823, and 479 cm–1. These peaks are designated to stretching vibration of unshared V–O, to bridging V−O−V vibrations and to the asymmetric stretching modes of the O shared between three vanadium atoms, respectively [43]. The C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous structure shows a less intense peak for V2O5 vibration with a broad absorption band appeared at a range of 2800–3000 cm–1 corresponds to the aromatic CC stretching vibration of C–dot, which indicates the well connection of C–dot with the V2O5. After calcination, peaks related to carbonyl and hydroxyl vibration disappeared in C–dotV2O5, which in turn confirms the removal of carboxyl functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of V2O5/GC and C–dot–V2O5/GC
ce:section.ce:para:
Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed using the different modified electrodes such as bare GC (a), V2O5/GC (b) and C–dot–V2O5/GC (c), in 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3–/4– electrolyte containing 0.1 M KCl with scan rate of 50 mVs–1 ( Fig. 6(A)). The CVs traces clearly show that all the three electrodes, bare GC, V2O5/GC and C–dot–V2O5/GC exhibited a peak–to–peak separation (ΔE p = E pa–E pc) values of 80 mV, 169 mV and 77 mV, respectively. Among the three electrode systems, the C–dot–V2O5/GC electrode revealed the low ΔE p (77 mV) and highest peak current intensity (56.6 μA). These indicates that the swift acceleration of [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– redox couple diffusing on electrode and electrolyte interface. CVs traces of underwent different sweep rate in [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– solution. The active surface area of the prepared electrodes was calculated using Randles–Sevcik Eq. (1) [44].

Where I p is the peak current, A is the surface area of the electrode (cm2), n is the number of electrons involved in the electrode reaction (n = 1), D 0 is the diffusion coefficient (D 0 =7.6 ×10–3 cm2 s–1), C 0 is the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– (C 0 = 5 mmol L–1), ν is the scan rate. The calculated active surface areas for bare GC (0.0741 cm2), V2O5/GC (0.0567 cm2). C–dot–V2O5/GC (0.0932 cm2) electrodes indicate that the C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous have high active surface area for electro–catalytic activity, which led to fast mass transport nature with the target sensor molecules than the bare and V2O5/GC electrodes.

Electron transfer properties of the bare and modified surface of the electrodes were analysed using EIS analysis. In general, a semicircle and linear segment are observed in Nyquist plot. The semicircle segment of the Nyquist plot appears at higher frequencies while the linear segment is observed at lower frequencies. The electron–limited transfer process causes the occurrence of the semicircle segment in the Nyquist plot. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) is given by the semicircle’s diameter. The linear segment can be attributed to the diffusion–controlled electron transfer process [45,46]. Fig. 6(B) shows the Nyquist plots of bare GC (a) V2O5/GC (b) C–dot–V2O5/GC (c) electrodes in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– redox–probe. The bare GC electrode shows Rct value of 1.13 kΩ while the V2O5/GC electrode shows Rct of 3.65 kΩ, because V2O5 nanorods inhibit the electron transfer kinetics of the redox–probe over modified electrode surface. In contrast, C–dot–V2O5/GC electrode reveals the Rct value of 0.56 kΩ, which is significantly reduced compare to V2O5, due to the more porous nature, electron density and active surface area of C–dot–V2O5 lattice surface which can facilitate fast charge transfer activity. Table 1 presented different electrode performances. The changes in the electrochemical properties suggest that the C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous was completely modified the GC electrode surface and also the C–dot–V2O5/GC electrode results in enhanced electrochemical activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical determination of Bisphenol A at C–dot–V2O5/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7(A) displayed CV curves of bare/GCE (a), V2O5/GCE (b), C–dot/GCE (c), and C–dot–V2O5/GCE (d) tailored electrodes recorded in the presence of 0.1 mM BPA under optimized 0.1 M PBS with in a potential window from 0.0 V to + 1.0 V. The CVs of the unmodified GCE electrode broad anodic peak current at + 0.65 V, which indicates poor electrochemical response towards the electro–oxidation of BPA. On the other hand, the V2O5 nanorods modified GCE electrode showed the oxidation peak at + 0.57 V. The electrochemical activity of V2O5 is responsible for the slight increment of oxidation peak current and reduced over potential. The C–dot/GCE modified electrode shows the BPA oxidation peak at + 0.6 V, which is lower than the bare GCE oxidation potential. However, the current intensity decreased, so C-dot alone on the GCE surface might cause instability. In differently, C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous modified electrode offered an enhanced anodic peak current of 28. 94 μA at + 0.55 V, which was 100 mV and 20 mV lower operating potential than bare GCE and V2O5 electrodes, respectively. This enhanced electrochemical signal of C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode could be attributed to high electrocatalytic activity as well as high active surface area [9].

Reduction peak was not observed during the reverse scan, it indicates that the electrochemical oxidative detection of BPA was an irreversible process. Among the four different modified electrodes, the C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode displayed a high electrochemical performance towards BPA detection, and the obtained electrochemical parameters are given in Table 1.

The different sweep rate (ν) of BPA on C–dot–V2O5/GC electrode were investigated by CV, in the presence of 0.1 mM BPA as shown in Fig. 7(B). This clearly shows that the anodic peak current of BPA (ipa) increased linearly with respect to the square root of the ν from 10 to 200 mVs–1 (Fig. 7(C)) and this linear regression was expressed as an Eq. (2).
(2)
i pa (μA) = 0.1951/2 (mV s–1) 1/2 + 0.272 (R2 = 0.9825)

The value of slope, in the above linear equation, indicates that the electro oxidation of BPA on the C–dot–V2O5/GC electrode surface is a diffusion–controlled process. The plot of log (ν) vs. E pa shown in Fig. 7(D) further confirms the electro oxidation of BPA. In the plot Fig. 7(D) shows a linear relation with a slope of 0. 537 which also represented in regression Eq. (3).
(3)
E pa (V) = 0.5372 V + 0.8961(mV s–1) (R2 = 0.9914)

Based on Laviron theory [46], the number of electron transferred (n) in the electrochemical oxidation of BPA at C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode were calculated using the following Eq. (4). The calculated number of electrons involved in the oxidation process to be 2.
(4) ip = nFQv4RT
ip – anodic peak current of BPA.
F – Faraday constant.
Q – charge Consumed.
V – scan rate.
R – gas constant.
T – temperature (K).

The irreversible electrode process, Epa is defined by Eq. (5) [46].
(5) Ep=E°+RTαnFlnRTksαnF+RTαnFlnv
E° – formal potential.
α – charge transfer coefficient.
n – number of the electrons transferred.
ks – electron transfer rate constant.
R – gas constant.
T – temperature (K).
F – Faraday constant.

Aforementioned equation, the α and K values are calculated from the slope of Epa versus ln (ν) and the measured slope was 0.537. By considering T = 298 K and substituting the values of R and F, the calculated charge transfer coefficient (α) and charge transfer rate constant (ks) were found to be to be 0.60 and 3.482 s–1, respectively.

The supporting electrolytes and their composition strongly affect the electrochemical response of the BPA [47]. With C–dot–V2O5/GCE as electrode, CVs was performed with 0.1 mM BPA under electrolyte pH ranging from 5.0 to 11.0. The different pH results BPA oxidation traces were shown in Fig. 8(A).

To increasing of pH from 5.0 to 7.0, the anodic peak current increased along with peak potential shifted towards low value. This is due to the presence of excess of H+ in the electrolyte at low pH, which tends to compete with those of the analyte. With the further increase the pH from 8.0 to 11, the anodic peak current decreased. The maximum peak current was observed at neutral pH medium of 7.0 (Fig. 8(B)), which was lower than the BPA pKa = 9.73 [48]. This is due to the adsorption of non–dissociated BPA on C–dot–V2O5/GCE surface [49]. Thus, effective electro–oxidation of BPA under 0.1 M PBS takes place at pH of 7.0. The slope of E pa verus pH was found to be 54 mV/pH (Fig. 8(C)), which was in line with the Nernst theoretical value of 59 mV/pH at 25 °C [49]. The closeness of the slopes indicates that the overall electrochemical process at the C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode was proton–dependent and equal number of electrons and protons were transferred electrode reaction [50].

The fabricated C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode was stabilized by CV process for 10 successive cyclic scans between –0.2–1.0 V. After that, amperometric technique was performed over C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode at a constant applied potential of + 0.55 V in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution under magnetically stir condition. The C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode showed almost zero current but after 60 s BPA 5.0 × 10–9 M addition into the supporting electrolyte, instant current (2 µA) increment was noticed ( Fig. 9 (A)). The current response increased for each successive addition of BPA for every 60 s as shown in Fig. 9 (A). The oxidation current increased gradually with respect to BPA concentration, starting from 5.0 × 10–9 M to 9.2 × 10–3 M (Fig. 9 (B)) due to the high surface area of the C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode. The linear calibration plot was yielded by plotting the concentration of BPA versus ipa with correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9982). The limits of detection were defined as 3σ/s (where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal (n = 8) and s is the slope of the calibration curve). By substituting the s and σ values, the obtained LOD to be 8.0 × 10–10 M and high sensitivity of 0.0681 μAμM–1 was achieved with the present sensor electrode. The electrochemical detection of BPA at C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode compared with recent reports and are listed in Table 2, which indicating that as fabricated C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode exhibited a wide concentration range and lowest BPA detection levels.

The selectivity of the C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrode was evaluated in the presence of potentially interfering species such as 5–fold higher concentrations of K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe3+ and Cl−, 2 folds of phenol, 4–acetaminophenol, 3–aminophenol, p–nitrophenol, p–nitroaniline, 2–aminophenol and 4–aminophenol, 2.5 folds of resorcinol, catechol and hydroquinone. Their presence had no interference in the detection of BPA at 0.55 V, as shown in Fig. 10 (A), as the detection exhibited relative error of less than ± 5 %. The relative errors are quite negligible, demonstrating that C–dot–V2O5/GCE has anti–interference ability and improved selectivity.

The reproducibility of five sequence C–dot–V2O5/GCE electrodes were estimated by replicate amperometric measurement using 50 μM of BPA in 0.1 M PBS. All the measured current values were found to be the same order with relative standard deviations (RSD) of 1.8 %, with respect to the original values, after 50 successive measurements. Long–term stability of as modified electrode was investigated for 1000 min (measurement were recorded for every 60 min) as shown in Fig. 10 (B). The resultant current response of the sensor decreased by only 5 %. This is likely due to the depletion of dissolved oxygen, close to the surface of the modified electrode. These observations indicate that the C–dot and V2O5/GCE electrodes have good repeatability, reproducibility, and extended stability.

The analytical capability of the fabricated sensor was analysed by sensing the BPA present in the various samples such as drinking water and milk samples by standard addition recovery test with interfering environmental substances. The modified electrode produced 97–100 % recovery with (n = 3). The modified electrode selectively detected the BPA and the obtained electroanalytical results given in Table 3. The obtained recoveries and relative standard deviations confirmed that the C–dot–V2O5 nanoporous modified electrode was potentially suitable for the determination of BPA in water and milk samples.

A novel fluorescent C–dot incorporated V2O5 layered nanoporous material was synthesised by thermal decomposition of C–dot with NH4VO3. The influence of C–dot were creating the porous structure of V2O5 layers which was analysed by various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The C–dot, electrostatically diffused into V2O5 matrix, under thermal condition which driven the V2O5 layers porous archistructure. Further, the fabricated C–dot–V2O5/GC electrode was used for the electrochemical detection of PBA in PBS buffer. The developed electrode revealed high electrochemical active surface area with good electron transfer rate and effective sensing activity at low operating potential. The proposed sensor exhibited enhanced sensitivity of 0.0681 μA μM–1 and detection limit of 8 × 10–10 M. Further, the practical applicability of the sensor electrode was demonstrated by BPA spiked drinking water and milk samples, the achieved outcomes are suitable for the clinical analysis.

Kuppusamy Rajesh: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Interpretation, Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Deivasigamani Ranjith Kumar: Investigation, editing. Balaji Bhargav P: Writing – review & editing. Manikandan R: Writing – review & editing. Nafis Ahmed: Formal analysis. Balaji C: Formal analysis. Jae–Jin Shim: Formal analysis.
